title: Four Million Records, One by One

narrator.personality: Quiet, methodical. Reports numbers -- consumed capacity, throttled requests, items scanned -- the way someone reads a weather report. The order backlog grows. They mention it.

story_beats.message: The order processing Lambda has failed 312 times in the last five minutes. The dead-letter queue has 287 messages. Customer service has six open chats. Nobody is asking about the search feature.
A senior engineer suggests the Lambda timeout might be set too low. It was raised from 15 seconds to 30 seconds last week and that fixed a similar-looking issue. The order backlog is at 1,400.
The order backlog is at 2,100. Someone in the incident channel asks if they should increase provisioned capacity on the table. Someone else asks if the search Lambda is still running.
The Lambda timeout is a symptom. The function is waiting on DynamoDB reads that never complete because the table is throttled. Something is consuming all the read capacity.

hints.text: The order processing was fine until 10:00 AM. Something else started hitting the table around that time.
Check CloudWatch for ConsumedReadCapacityUnits. Something is reading far more than the order pipeline ever would.
The product-search Lambda was deployed at 9:58 AM. Look at how it reads from the table.
There is a difference between a DynamoDB Scan and a Query. One reads every item in the table. The other uses an index.
The search Lambda runs a Scan with a FilterExpression on the 'category' attribute. There is no GSI on category. Every invocation reads all four million items.

glossary.Scan: A DynamoDB operation that reads every item in a table from start to finish. It always touches every row, even if you only want a few. Expensive on large tables.
glossary.Query: A DynamoDB operation that reads only the items matching a specific partition key. Much faster and cheaper than a Scan because it skips everything that does not match.
glossary.Global Secondary Index (GSI): A separate lookup structure on a DynamoDB table that lets you query by a different attribute than the table's main key. For example, querying products by category instead of by product ID.
glossary.Read Capacity Unit (RCU): A unit of read throughput for a DynamoDB table. One RCU gives you one strongly consistent read per second for an item up to 4 KB. When you run out, reads get throttled.
glossary.Provisioned Throughput: A capacity mode where you set a fixed number of reads and writes per second in advance. If traffic exceeds what you provisioned, DynamoDB rejects the extra requests.
glossary.FilterExpression: A setting on a Scan or Query that removes items from the results after they have already been read. It does not reduce the amount of data DynamoDB reads or the capacity consumed.
glossary.ProvisionedThroughputExceededException: The error DynamoDB returns when a request tries to read or write but there is no remaining capacity. It means the table is throttled.
glossary.Dead-Letter Queue (DLQ): A queue where messages go when they fail processing after all retries. In this sim, failed orders land in the DLQ because the Lambda cannot write to the throttled table.
glossary.Partition Key: The primary attribute DynamoDB uses to decide which internal partition stores an item. A good partition key distributes data evenly across partitions.

narrative_arc.call: Tuesday morning. A new search feature was deployed two minutes ago. Orders are still flowing. Nothing looks wrong yet.
narrative_arc.threshold: ConsumedReadCapacityUnits hits 100 -- the provisioned ceiling. The order-writer Lambda starts failing. The dead-letter queue begins to fill. The two events look unrelated.
narrative_arc.trials: A senior engineer raises the Lambda timeout. It does not help. Someone suggests increasing provisioned capacity. The backlog keeps climbing. The search feature returns results slowly, which makes it look functional, not culpable.
narrative_arc.revelation: The product-search Lambda performs a full table Scan on every invocation. Four million items, read cover to cover, each time a customer searches by category. The FilterExpression discards most of them after the read. The capacity was consumed before the filter ran.
narrative_arc.return: The search Lambda is disabled. Throttling stops within sixty seconds. The lasting fix is a Global Secondary Index on the category attribute and a Query operation instead of a Scan.

components.name: API Gateway (tidepool-api)
components.role: Routes /search and /orders requests to their respective Lambda functions
components.failure_impact: If the API Gateway fails, both search and order operations are unavailable

components.name: Lambda: tidepool-product-search (v14)
components.role: Newly deployed search function that performs full table Scans with FilterExpression on the category attribute
components.failure_impact: Each invocation consumes all provisioned RCUs by scanning 4 million items, starving other consumers

components.name: Lambda: tidepool-order-writer (v9)
components.role: Existing order pipeline function that writes confirmed orders via PutItem
components.failure_impact: When throttled by RCU exhaustion, orders fail and are sent to the dead-letter queue

components.name: DynamoDB table: tidepool-products
components.role: Stores 4,012,387 product items, provisioned at 100 RCU / 100 WCU, no Global Secondary Indexes
components.failure_impact: When all RCUs are consumed, every reader and writer receives ProvisionedThroughputExceededException

components.name: SQS dead-letter queue: tidepool-orders-dlq
components.role: Receives order messages that fail processing after all retries
components.failure_impact: Messages in the DLQ represent lost orders that require manual reprocessing

components.name: CloudWatch alarm
components.role: Fires when ThrottledRequests exceeds 10 for 5 consecutive minutes
components.failure_impact: If the alarm is not acted on, throttling continues and the order backlog grows

system_narration.data_flow: Customer search request arrives at API Gateway, routes to tidepool-product-search Lambda, which runs a Scan on tidepool-products with a FilterExpression. The Scan paginates through all 4 million items in 1 MB pages, consuming RCUs proportional to total table size. Simultaneously, order requests route to tidepool-order-writer, which attempts PutItem and Query operations on the same table. When the Scan exhausts all 100 provisioned RCUs, the order-writer receives ProvisionedThroughputExceededException, retries three times, then sends the order to the dead-letter queue.
system_narration.what_broke: The product-search Lambda uses Scan + FilterExpression instead of Query + GSI. A Scan reads every item regardless of the filter. On a 4-million-item table with 100 provisioned RCUs, a single paginated Scan consumes the entire read budget. Multiple concurrent searches compound the problem. Every other reader of the table -- including the order pipeline -- is starved of capacity.

resolution.root_cause: Lambda function uses DynamoDB Scan with a FilterExpression instead of Query with a proper partition key or GSI, consuming all provisioned RCUs on a 4-million-item table.

fix_criteria.description: Player identifies that the product-search Lambda is performing a full table Scan instead of a Query
Player proposes creating a Global Secondary Index on the search attribute (category) to enable Query operations
Player explains that the Scan consumed all 100 provisioned RCUs, causing throttling for every other consumer of the table
Player recognizes that the Lambda timeout errors are a symptom of DynamoDB throttling, not a root cause

learning_objectives: DynamoDB Scan reads every item in the table and consumes RCUs proportional to total table size, regardless of FilterExpression
A FilterExpression does not reduce the read capacity consumed -- it only filters results after the data is read
DynamoDB Query uses partition key (and optionally sort key) to read only matching items, consuming far fewer RCUs
Global Secondary Indexes enable Query access patterns on non-key attributes
When provisioned RCUs are exhausted, all consumers of the table experience ProvisionedThroughputExceededException
sop_steps: Confirm the table is overwhelmed. Check CloudWatch metrics for the table -- look at ConsumedReadCapacityUnits (how many reads are happening) and ThrottledRequests (how many reads are being rejected). If consumed reads equal the maximum you set, the table is at capacity and rejecting overflow.

Figure out what is consuming all the reads. Compare the time when throttling started with any recent code deployments or new features. Check CloudWatch Logs and deployment timestamps to find what changed right before things broke.

Look at the code. Read the Lambda function to see how it reads from the table. A Scan reads every single item in the table from start to finish. A Query reads only the items that match a specific key. If the code uses Scan with a FilterExpression, it is reading everything and then throwing away what it does not need -- wasting enormous amounts of read capacity.

Stop the immediate damage. Disable the Lambda that is doing the Scan by removing its trigger from API Gateway (the service that routes web requests to it) or by setting its allowed concurrent executions to 0. Watch the ThrottledRequests metric drop to zero.

Process the backlog. Orders that failed while the table was overwhelmed were sent to a dead-letter queue (a holding area for failed messages). Once throttling stops, re-process those messages so the orders go through.

Build a shortcut for lookups. Create a Global Secondary Index (GSI) -- think of it as a second table of contents organized by a different attribute. Use the searched attribute (category) as the main lookup key and productName as a secondary sort key. This lets the database jump directly to matching items instead of reading everything.

Rewrite the code to use the shortcut. Replace the Scan + FilterExpression call with a Query + KeyConditionExpression against the new GSI. A Query tells the database exactly what to look for, so it reads only the matching items. Test with production-scale data to confirm it works.
Verify the fix reduced read consumption. Monitor ConsumedReadCapacityUnits after redeployment. The Query should consume a tiny fraction of what the Scan was using.

Consider switching to automatic scaling. The table currently has a fixed read budget (called provisioned capacity). On-demand capacity mode scales automatically based on traffic, which prevents this kind of exhaustion -- though it costs more per individual read.

Add early warning systems. Set up CloudWatch alarms on ConsumedReadCapacityUnits and ThrottledRequests so you get notified within minutes of capacity exhaustion, not after customers complain. Require load testing against production-scale data for any new Lambda that reads from shared tables.

related_failure_modes.scenario: One popular item gets all the traffic and overwhelms its storage partition
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: In this sim, the Scan uses up all the read capacity for the entire table. A 'hot partition' problem is different: the total capacity is not exhausted, but one specific group of data gets so much traffic that its individual slice of the database cannot keep up. Reads to that one key get throttled while the rest of the table is fine. The monitoring looks confusing because overall consumption appears normal.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Choose a partition key (the attribute DynamoDB uses to organize data internally) that spreads traffic evenly -- like a unique ID rather than a status code. Use CloudWatch Contributor Insights to detect which keys are getting the most traffic.

related_failure_modes.scenario: A secondary index cannot keep up with writes, and that slows down the main table too
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: In this sim, there is no secondary index and reads are the problem. This scenario is about writes. When you add a Global Secondary Index (GSI), DynamoDB must update that index every time you write to the main table. If the index does not have enough write capacity, DynamoDB throttles writes to the main table itself to avoid the index falling behind. Your main table has plenty of capacity, but writes still fail.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Give the GSI enough write capacity to keep up with the main table. Monitor the metric called IndexWriteProvisionedThroughputExceeded. Consider on-demand capacity mode if write patterns are unpredictable.

related_failure_modes.scenario: An on-demand table hits its automatic scaling limit during a sudden traffic spike
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: This sim uses a fixed read budget (provisioned mode). On-demand mode removes the fixed budget and scales automatically, but it still has a limit: traffic cannot more than double within a 30-minute window. A brand-new feature that suddenly generates heavy traffic can hit this ceiling even though there is no manually set limit.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Gradually ramp up traffic before launching a new feature so the on-demand scaling can keep pace. Set a maximum throughput limit as a cost safety net. Monitor for throttling with the reason MaxOnDemandThroughputExceeded.

related_failure_modes.scenario: A scheduled batch job reads the entire table and starves other operations
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: This sim has a user-facing function running Scans on every customer request. A batch job (for analytics, exports, or data backfills) causes the same capacity exhaustion, but it happens on a schedule rather than after a deployment. The symptoms appear periodically -- every night at 2 AM, for example -- rather than suddenly.
related_failure_modes.prevention: When you must read the entire table for a batch job, read it in small pages with deliberate pauses between each page. This spreads the read load over time instead of consuming everything at once. Better yet, run batch jobs against a read replica or a separate copy of the table.

sop_practices: Design your table so that everyday lookups use Query (which goes straight to the matching items) instead of Scan (which reads the entire table). Scan should be reserved for rare administrative tasks, never for user-facing features.

Before deploying any new function that reads from a shared DynamoDB table, test it against production-scale data volumes. A query that works fine on 1,000 test items can cripple a table with 4 million items.

Set up CloudWatch alarms on ConsumedReadCapacityUnits (how much of your read budget is being used) and ThrottledRequests (how many reads are being rejected). You want to know about capacity problems within minutes, not after customers start complaining.

During code reviews, watch for FilterExpression used with Scan. A FilterExpression only filters results after the entire table has already been read -- it does not save any read capacity. If you see this pattern, it almost always means the code needs a Global Secondary Index (a secondary lookup structure) and a Query instead.
